lesson.menu_desc: Connectors of cause and result — because, so, since and as a result: the server was overloaded, so requests timed out.
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 — Cause and Result (Connectors)
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Daniela Feitoza | Aula 18 -- Cause and Result

listenings.text: Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Today, listen to one short voice note about explaining cause and result. When you connect a problem to its effect, you need linking words. The two most common are because and so. Use because before the cause. The service crashed because the server was overloaded. Use so before the result. The server was overloaded, so the service crashed. Notice the order. Because plus cause. So plus result. You can also use since, which means the same as because. Since the latency was high, requests timed out. For a more formal result, use as a result or therefore. The capacity was too low. As a result, the system slowed down. So, because and since point to the cause, and so, therefore and as a result point to the effect. Practice with a real incident. Explain what caused the problem and what the result was. Talk soon.

Hello Daniela, this is Marcus. I am leaving you a quick voicemail before the stakeholder briefing. I need you to explain the cause and the result of the slowdown clearly. So, here is the chain. There was a sudden surge in traffic, so the server reached its capacity. Because the load passed the threshold, the latency went up. As a result, requests started to time out. The real cause was a dependency on a slow database. Since that database had no redundancy, one failure caused congestion across the whole system. We had to throttle the requests to protect it. The lesson is clear. Because we lacked resilience, a small overload became a big outage. In the briefing, link each cause to its result with because, so and as a result. Stakeholders love a clear story. Call me back when you can. Thanks, Daniela. Talk soon.
